caveats: a: Specialty-crop robotics evidence is not representative of already mechanized grain and forage crews.
a: The dataset labor ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment, so it may misstate the current labor share even though it is not re-estimated here.
rho: Successful demonstrations may not persist through dust, darkness, lodging, mud, crop variability, and time-critical commercial workloads.
rho: Implementation economics vary sharply by crop, terrain, fleet age, annual machine hours, and ability to redeploy equipment across regions.
e: NAICS assignment is establishment-based and may not cleanly separate custom harvesting from diversified farming, hauling, planting, or manure services.
e: The LMM firm count is a margin-bridged estimate from size-class receipts rather than an observed count.
s5: Association leadership profiles are selected anecdotes and not a representative owner-age or transaction sample.
s5: Equipment liquidation, internal family succession, and retirement without a transferable operation are not qualifying control transfers.
q: State surveys mix farmers, custom operators, cooperatives, and other service providers and do not isolate LMM firms.
q: Pricing power varies with harvest urgency, local crew scarcity, crop condition, field size, haul distance, and customer concentration.
d5: The USDA baseline covers major field crops rather than the full NAICS service basket.
d5: Acreage does not directly measure outsourced harvest intensity, crop mix, machine hours, or value of timeliness.
o: Continued physical harvesting does not guarantee that an independent operator of today's kind remains the supplier.
o: Autonomy could shift accountability toward manufacturers, dealers, or large farm platforms even when field work remains physical.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are farms, ranches, dairies, landowners, and crop producers that outsource seasonal harvest operations; revenue is usually charged per acre, bale, ton, hour, crop unit, or bundled harvested-and-hauled job, with repeat seasonal relationships and the contractor supplying machinery and operators.
lens.excluded_activities: A farm harvesting only its own crops, equipment-only rental, hand-harvest labor contracting without a substantive machine-harvesting service, crop production for the operator's own account, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market custom harvesting firms that repeatedly supply machine-based combining, picking, threshing, mowing, baling, forage chopping, and closely related harvest and field-haul services to external farms and producers.

a.bridge: USDA sources document autonomous systems, machine vision, yield sensing, harvest robotics, and computer-controlled platforms across farms and specialty crops, but do not measure the wage-weighted not-yet-automated task share for LMM custom harvesters. I map the exposed driving, machine-setting, routing, dispatch, crop-condition assessment, documentation, and office tasks to this industry's labor base; prototype emphasis and already-common mechanization make the proxy more likely to overstate remaining substitution.
caveats: Specialty-crop robotics evidence is not representative of already mechanized grain and forage crews.
The dataset labor ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment, so it may misstate the current labor share even though it is not re-estimated here.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study from at least twenty custom grain, forage, cotton, and specialty-crop harvest firms, separated by existing guidance and autonomy adoption, would materially change this share.
a.rationale: AI can improve route and crew scheduling, autonomous or supervised machine operation, crop and moisture recognition, machine settings, predictive maintenance, yield documentation, quoting, billing, and customer communication. Repair, transport, machine setup, field recovery, safety oversight, irregular-condition judgment, and physical handling remain substantial and difficult to remove.

d5.bridge: USDA projects a modest contraction in planted acreage for eight major US field crops over the coming decade, while the screened construct is five-year constant-price demand for all machine-based outsourced harvest services. I translate the acreage trajectory into roughly stable service quantity, allowing outsourcing gains, specialty crops, forage, multiple passes, and regional migration to offset part or all of field-crop acreage decline; the major-crop proxy may understate diversified harvest demand but overstate it if farms consolidate and self-perform.
caveats: The USDA baseline covers major field crops rather than the full NAICS service basket.
Acreage does not directly measure outsourced harvest intensity, crop mix, machine hours, or value of timeliness.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast combining USDA harvested acreage by crop with the share custom harvested, passes per acre, regional route utilization, forage tonnage, and specialty-crop machine adoption would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Crops still must be harvested each season, but aggregate field-crop acreage is projected to soften. Demand for contractors can hold up better than acreage when machinery costs rise, crews are scarce, farms need surge capacity, or specialists spread expensive machines over more acres, while consolidation and improved equipment productivity work in the opposite direction.

e.bridge: The Census classification and industry association describe establishments and members whose core activity is machine harvesting for farmers, which closely matches the external recurring-service lens, but neither directly measures the share of dataset-estimated LMM firms that are active, independent, externally serving, and transferable. Because NAICS 115113 is itself a support-service code, the proxy should be close, with residual risk more likely to make it overstate eligibility through farm side businesses and misclassified equipment rental.
caveats: NAICS assignment is establishment-based and may not cleanly separate custom harvesting from diversified farming, hauling, planting, or manure services.
The LMM firm count is a margin-bridged estimate from size-class receipts rather than an observed count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 25 dataset-estimated LMM businesses showing external-customer revenue share, ultimate ownership, normalized EBITDA, operating status, and separability from affiliated farms would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Custom harvesting is inherently an outsourced service, and repeat seasonal customers are central to fleet utilization. Most active independent firms in the LMM band should therefore fit, with exclusions for captive affiliates, businesses primarily farming their own land, equipment rental, or firms whose economics are inseparable from a farm operation.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 115113 ($559,573,751) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 115113 ($810,534,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $297,662 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 115113); class <5: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $529,587 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $42,579 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,852,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $148,901 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,286,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $264,246 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,109,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,214,828 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,958,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,408,655 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 25

caveats: Continued physical harvesting does not guarantee that an independent operator of today's kind remains the supplier.
Autonomy could shift accountability toward manufacturers, dealers, or large farm platforms even when field work remains physical.
o.change_evidence: Customer surveys and OEM channel plans quantifying planned self-harvesting, autonomous equipment-as-a-service, dealer-operated fleets, and direct software substitution among current custom-harvest users would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Harvesting remains a physical, time-critical field operation requiring accountable ownership of machines, mobilization, safety, maintenance, crop-loss judgment, and customer coordination. AI can reduce labor per machine and change crew structure but cannot make the service software-only; displacement instead comes from farmers buying equipment, autonomous equipment offered directly by OEMs or dealers, or consolidation into captive fleets.

q.bridge: State custom-rate surveys observe competitive charges that bundle machinery, power, fuel, and the operator, but do not measure the discounted share of AI-enabled benefit retained by LMM custom harvesters. I infer that fixed per-acre and per-unit quotes preserve gains within a season, then discount for transparent rate benchmarks, annual negotiation, fuel and equipment-cost pass-through, and customers' ability to self-perform; public benchmarks likely make retention lower than in opaque specialist services.
caveats: State surveys mix farmers, custom operators, cooperatives, and other service providers and do not isolate LMM firms.
Pricing power varies with harvest urgency, local crew scarcity, crop condition, field size, haul distance, and customer concentration.
q.change_evidence: Multi-year customer contracts, quote logs, realized rates, margin bridges, and renewal outcomes from custom harvesters before and after guidance, autonomy, or scheduling deployments would materially change retention.
q.rationale: A contractor can initially keep fuel, labor, routing, uptime, and throughput gains when the job price is fixed per acre or output unit. Retention erodes as farmers compare published custom rates, negotiate recurring work, purchase their own machinery, or expect improved throughput and quality in the base service.

rho.bridge: USDA research and impact pages show functioning automation, computer vision, and autonomous agricultural systems, including measured gains in selected specialty-crop settings, but not five-year implementation of the exposed opportunity in US LMM custom-harvest businesses. I discount those demonstrations for capital intensity, multi-brand fleets, rural connectivity, safety, weather, crop variability, customer field data, and limited technical staff; research projects probably overstate near-term fleet-wide implementation.
caveats: Successful demonstrations may not persist through dust, darkness, lodging, mud, crop variability, and time-critical commercial workloads.
Implementation economics vary sharply by crop, terrain, fleet age, annual machine hours, and ability to redeploy equipment across regions.
rho.change_evidence: Dealer and OEM records for US custom-harvester installations, paired with operator interviews on adoption, uptime, acres per operator, intervention rates, installed cost, and payback, would move this range.
rho.rationale: Guidance, telematics, optimization, computer vision, and office automation can be layered onto modern fleets and should diffuse faster than fully driverless harvesting. Full autonomy is constrained by moving crews, public-road transport, variable fields, breakdown response, narrow harvest windows, machine compatibility, safety liability, and poor economics for short seasonal utilization.

s5.bridge: The US Custom Harvesters association profiles document family operations, next-generation participation, independent startups, equipment sales, and changes in owner operating roles, but not qualifying control transfers or a five-year transfer rate for LMM firms. I treat this as evidence of succession pressure and transferable fleets and relationships; anecdotes probably overstate continuity through family succession and do not reveal the share sold to outside control buyers.
caveats: Association leadership profiles are selected anecdotes and not a representative owner-age or transaction sample.
Equipment liquidation, internal family succession, and retirement without a transferable operation are not qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of agricultural broker listings, equipment-auction records, UCC releases, PitchBook and Capital IQ transactions, plus confidential succession surveys of USCHI members, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Expensive mobile fleets, repeat customers, trained crews, and regional routes can transfer, while family ownership and generational businesses create succession events. However, firms may sell equipment piecemeal, shrink into owner-operation, transfer internally, or cease when customer relationships and operator skill do not survive the founder, limiting qualifying control sales.

principal_driver: A high-fit outsourced service model where scarce crews and expensive mobile fleets create recurring demand for productivity and scheduling improvements.
principal_weakness: Already-mechanized work and capital-intensive, safety-critical field conditions limit both remaining AI labor exposure and rapid full-autonomy adoption.
